Curriculum → Data storage and indexing → Partitioning, federation, and sharding

Lesson 3 of 5

Partitioning, federation, and sharding

Break data into smaller pieces when a single machine or logical schema becomes the bottleneck for size, throughput, or ownership.

25-35 min Data storage and indexing 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

Partitioning strategies are common interview deep dives because they expose trade-offs around hot keys, joins, and operational mobility.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Partition for a reason Horizontal partitioning spreads load across shards2. Choose stable keys carefully User ID often works for user-owned data3. Accept the operational costs Plan resharding before you need it urgently

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing Partitioning strategies are common interview deep dives because they expose trade-offs around hot keys, joins, and operational mobility. 2. Partition for a reason Horizontal partitioning spreads load across shards3. Choose stable keys carefully User ID often works for user-owned data4. Accept the operational costs Plan resharding before you need it urgently
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

Partitioning strategies are common interview deep dives because they expose trade-offs around hot keys, joins, and operational mobility.

What to say first

  • State why a single store is no longer enough.
  • Choose and justify a partition key.
  • Explain cross-shard query limitations and mitigation.
2

Study section

Partition for a reason

Partitioning helps when throughput or storage exceeds a single node, or when separate domains need independent lifecycle management.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Horizontal partitioning spreads load across shards
  • Federation separates functional domains into different databases
  • Archive or cold-storage partitions can reduce hot-set pressure
3

Study section

Choose stable keys carefully

A good shard key distributes load and keeps common queries local. A bad key creates hot partitions or expensive scatter-gather reads.

Call these points out explicitly

  • User ID often works for user-owned data
  • Time-based keys can create hot partitions without bucketing
  • Consistent hashing helps when capacity changes frequently
4

Study section

Accept the operational costs

Cross-shard transactions, rebalancing, backfills, and global secondary indexes become harder after partitioning.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Plan resharding before you need it urgently
  • Keep routing metadata highly available
  • Model hot-spot mitigation explicitly

Reference diagram

Visual anchor

Shard keys should distribute load while keeping the common request path local.

Adapted from The System Design Primer by Donne Martin (CC BY 4.0).

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • State why a single store is no longer enough.
  • Choose and justify a partition key.
  • Explain cross-shard query limitations and mitigation.
  • Mention rebalancing and hot-partition handling.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Sharding before the access pattern is understood.
  • Choosing a shard key that aligns with traffic bursts.
  • Ignoring metadata, rebalancing, and cross-shard aggregations.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • How would you shard a timeline service?
  • What is the difference between federation and sharding?
  • When is consistent hashing useful?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Relational data modeling and indexing Data storage and indexingCaching layers and cache placement Caching, asynchrony, and resilience

Simulation lab

Simulation lab: Partitioning, federation, and sharding

Use the simulator to see when primary, replica, shard, or queue pressure should change your storage story. Apply it to partitioning, federation, and sharding so the trade-offs feel concrete.

Use this lab to turn "Partitioning, federation, and sharding" into a measurable system instead of a static definition.Pressure the path around partition for a reason and watch how it interacts with choose stable keys carefully.Partitioning strategies are common interview deep dives because they expose trade-offs around hot keys, joins, and operational mobility.

Choose an API path

Which flow are you stressing?

Focus metrics

  • read p95
  • replica utilization
  • shard pressure
  • cache hit ratio

Workload profile

Traffic shape

Signals

What the editor found

  • Line 1: edge · 100000 rps · 4 ms
  • Line 2: service · 38000 rps · 10 ms
  • Line 3: cache · 120000 rps · 3 ms
  • Line 4: database · 8500 rps · 20 ms
  • Line 5: database · 17000 rps · 16 ms
  • Line 6: database · 21000 rps · 18 ms

Built-in runtime adapters

Browser and WebAssembly execution targets

No eval

The simulator runs its DSL interpreters in-browser today and now exposes adapter slots for WebAssembly-backed runtimes like Pyodide or custom interpreters.

Native flow-graph compiler

Runs the topology and simulation DSL interpreters directly in the browser.

Available now
Pyodide / Python WASM adapter

Ready for browser-side Python interpreters compiled to WebAssembly.

Register adapter
Lua / custom WASM adapter

Use this slot for additional in-browser interpreters backed by WebAssembly modules.

Register adapter

Good first experiments

  • State why a single store is no longer enough.
  • Choose and justify a partition key.
  • Explain cross-shard query limitations and mitigation.

Interactive diagram

Compiled simulation topology

Rendering diagram…

This preview is generated from the current flow-graph input.

API modeling notes

How this simulator thinks

  • Start with the critical path from Partition for a reason and use the metrics to explain where latency or capacity moves.
  • Use the simulator to justify checklist items like State why a single store is no longer enough. before you propose scaling changes.
  • Treat the async queue and worker path as the escape hatch for ideas from Choose stable keys carefully.

What you get after a run

Simulation output

  • Per-node utilization, queue depth, and dropped traffic
  • Average, p95, and p99 latency for the selected API path
  • Probable fixes mapped to the dominant bottleneck

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later without needing automated grading.

0/3 prompts saved Prompt 1 of 3

Prompt flow

What you will practice

interview

Explain the concept

Not saved yet

Teach "Partitioning, federation, and sharding" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal for this step

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Use these guardrails

  • How would you shard a timeline service?
  • Partition for a reason
  • Choose stable keys carefully
  • State why a single store is no longer enough.

Suggested structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs

Bring your own model
